reasoning: B is strongest: it separates the causal claim from the product claim, enumerates five rival causes including multiple-comparisons, and uniquely supplies an outside-view base rate (the vitamin E / beta-carotene / HRT reversal graveyard) with explicit probabilities (~20-25% causal, <10% supplement, 75-80% claim overstated) plus a Mendelian-randomization falsifier, giving it the best calibration and depth. A is nearly as rigorous and very clean (the 'precise estimate of a biased number' point, relative-vs-absolute math, reverse causation, ingredient-substitution leap, conflict of interest) but stays qualitative on uncertainty. C reasons well and grounds itself in named external sources and FDA guidance, but leans on specific cited statistics I cannot verify (confident specificity risk), gives less quantified uncertainty, and omits the multiple-comparisons angle.
